text: The surgeon uses the lower pole of the kidney and the intersection with iliac vessels as anatomical markers to achieve a precise overlay of the virtual ureter onto the real ureter during augmented reality navigation.
Augmented reality technology is utilized to overlay a 3D model of the ureter and its stone onto the operative field, allowing for precise visualization of the ureteral stricture location.
The surgeon identifies the stricture level with the embedded stone and proceeds to incise the ureter, resecting the stenotic ureteral tract.

The surgeon performs ureteroscopy to visually confirm the accuracy of the augmented reality model superimposed over the ureter, ensuring the virtual representation aligns with the actual anatomical structures.
The surgeon prepares an omental flap, which will be used in the subsequent reconstructive phase of the procedure.
The augmented reality system superimposes the virtual model of the ureter and the stone onto the real anatomy, aligning the digital representation with the visible structures in the operative field.
The surgeon identifies the ureteral stricture and makes an incision to resect the stenotic ureteral tract, preserving a significant portion of the healthy peristenotic ureter.
During the surgical resection, the embedded stone is clearly visible within the ureter, allowing for precise identification and removal.
Microcalcifications are visible on the ureteral wall at the current level of dissection.
Indocyanine green (ICG) dye is injected through the nephrostomy tube, but the fluorescence does not provide clear visual feedback regarding the level of the ureteral stricture.
The surgeon cuts and spatulates the ureter, preparing it for the reconstructive phase of the procedure.
The surgeon prepares a buccal mucosal graft, which is visibly ready for use before the robotic phase of the surgery begins.
The surgeon contours a three by one centimeter surface of the oral mucosa, preparing it for grafting.
The surgeon uses forceps to cut the mucosal graft, preparing it for further use in the procedure.
The surgeon fits the prepared buccal mucosal graft into the surgical site, ensuring proper alignment and placement.
The surgeon places the mucosal graft into the abdominal cavity after completing the posterior plate of the anastomosis with a terminal-terminal suture.
The surgeon applies the mucosal graft over the defect and sutures it to the peripheral free margins of the anastomized ureteral segment, ensuring there is no tension on the suture.
The surgeon models the graft tissue over the ureteral defect, ensuring it covers the area completely for repair.
The surgeon inserts an instrument into the newly reconstructed ureter, demonstrating the increased caliber that facilitates its progression through the ureteral segment.
The surgeon wraps the omental flap, prepared earlier in the procedure, around the repaired ureter to ensure graft vascularization and prevent stricture recurrence.
